title: practical english usage - Part 295
content:
He gets to the market by bike.

-   He works at the market.

movement or direction. Compare: At and in are generally used for position (for the difference, ►    384); to is used for 1  the   difference 385  at/in    and   to For the difference between at/in and to, ►    385.   For smile at, shoot at, etc, ►    385.3.

Note that at is usually pronounced /ət/, not /æt/  (►   315).

on a farm working on the railway in bed / (the) hospital / prison but The map is on page 32. (    I stopped reading at page 32.) in a tent   in a hat in a picture         in the sky               in the rain in/at church  at home/work in/at school/college Note these expressions: 7 special     expressions I used to get my bread at a small baker's in the next street.

'Where's Megan?" ‘ She's at Naomi's.' 'at somebody’ s house or shop’ .

At can be used with a possessive (especially in British English) to mean vocabulaiy •  386   at, on and in: time We're having the roof repaired at Easter.

Easter and Thanksgiving (AmE).

We   use at to talk about the whole of the holidays at Christmas, New  Year, at 4 public     holidays      and   weekends: We usually go and see Granny on Sundays.

We   use plurals (Sundays, Mondays, etc) to talk about repeated actions.

I'm seeing her Sunday morning.

In an informal style we  sometimes leave out on.

They're having a party on Christmas Day.

III ring you on Tuesday.  My birthday's on March 21st.

We met on a cold afternoon in early spring.

See you on Monday morning.

describe the morning/afternoon, etc.

We   use on if we  say which  morning/afternoon, etc we  are talking about, or if we Would you rather work days or nights?

In an informal style, we  sometimes use plurals (days, etc) with no preposition.

I had to get up in the night. I often work at night.

particular night') and at night (= during any night). Compare: Note the difference between in the night (mostly used to mean ‘ during one We usually go out in the evening.

I work best in the morning. three o'clock in the afternoon 2 in parts    of  the   day: What time does your train leave?

At is usually left out before what time in an informal style (►  386.7).

Phone me at lunch time.

I usually get up at six o'clock.  I'll meet you at 4.15.

at + weekend, public holiday at + clock time 386 at,   on   and   in:  time For in and into, ►    496.

When did you arrive in New Zealand? (     ...-to- New-Zealand?) not We should arrive at Pat's in time for lunch, (     ... arrive to Pat-*...) Arrive is generally followed by at or in; never by to.

'The train's late again,' she said, pointing to the timetable.

Word  Problems from A  to Z      Section 31 vocabulary •  387   at all She was hardly frightened at all.

I didn't understand anything at all. (= ɪ  didn’ t understand even a little.) We   often use at all to emphasise a negative idea.

1 at   all   with    a  negative 387 at   all For the difference between in and during, ►    441.

Note that at is usually pronounced /ət/, not /æt/   (►  315).

'What time are you leaving?' 'Eight o'clock.' Which week did you say you're away?

What day is the meeting?

expression of time, and in answers which  only contain an expression of time.

And prepositions are usually dropped in questions beginning What/ Which + What are you doing the day after tomorrow?

yesterday, tomorrow or the day after tomorrow.

These prepositions are not normally used, either, before yesterday, the day before We stayed all day.

I'm at home every evening. We met one day in late August.

Are you free this morning?  I didn't feel very well that week.

See you next week.      Come any time.

(sometimes), one, any (in an informal style), each, every, some, all.

At/on/in are not normally used in expressions of time before next, last, this, that 7 expressions         with    no   preposition I haven't seen her in years.

periods up to the present.

In American English, in can be used in negative sentences, like for, to talk about not He wrote the book in a month. (     ... in a month's time.) It'll be ready in three weeks' time.

I'll see you again in a month's time.
